data.title: Micron's AI Supercycle Accelerates
data.description:
Investment Thesis

At the core of the AI memory bottleneck is Micron Technology (MU). Since my last coverage when I called the supercyle starting point, MU had a strong bull run rising by more than 45%. Their HBM3E and their next-gen HBM4 products are shipping into high-volume production with their improved speed and power efficiency, which is exactly what hyperscalers are demanding.

With 2026 High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) supply sold out and contracts locked in for multi-year supply agreements, Micron Technology is in a great position to drive their margins significantly higher as their premium mix of memory products could increase their margins by an astonishing 99% in FY26.

HBM4 Ramp Drives 99% EBITDA Growth Into FY26

I see that Micron has a commanding market lead based on the intersection of high-volume production of advanced memory products and an aggregate demand status that exceeds global supply. The main part of this financial setup is the HBM product line. I am mainly pointing to HBM3E and the newer HBM4 generations. Micron has commenced high-volume manufacturing of HBM4 with shipments ramping in Q1-26. This timeline is an acceleration occurring one quarter earlier than Micron management earlier guided. These products may directly lead to financial leverage. The HBM4 product achieves speeds of over 11 gigabits per second.

Furthermore, the HBM3E product achieved 30% less power consumption than that of the competitors' products. This efficiency of the product is critical for data centers that are focusing on managing energy costs associated with AI workloads. Besides HBM, Micron has also come up with a new product, Low Power DRAM (LPDRAM), which has been applied in server configurations. This type of product has 60% lower power consumption compared to conventional DDR memory. This has encouraged data center architects to adopt Micron's products.

I believe this product lead is in line with a supercycle led by AI demand. The expansion of AI models that need longer context windows needs higher performance memory and storage. As per Micron’s management, server demand growth accelerated from single digits to high-teens percentages. As a result, the data center solid-state drive (SSD) business hit a $1 billion run rate. Strong demand across these high-value categories leads to margin expansion.

For that, Micron guided gross margins to 68%. This margin reflects a favorable pricing environment coming from tight supply conditions. I think this tightness may continue beyond 2026. As prices increase at these elevated margin levels, the impact on profitability becomes lesser on a percentage basis but leads heavily to absolute earnings. Micron’s TTM Gross Profit Margin is at 45.31% and it has an EBIT Margin of 32.55% that is 396.33% above the sector median. I think this is pointing out that Micron Technology captures value sharply from its topline.

Moreover, the forward metrics hold this positive momentum. Revenue growth forward is 57.25% that is 461% higher than the sector median with forward EBITDA growth is projected at 99.38%. These levels indicate that the market expects the pricing environment and product mix to result in large expansion in financials. The conversion of topline to bottomline performance is visible in the forward EPS diluted growth of 225%.

Micron has secured multiyear agreements with clients. These arrangements include specific volume commitments. These are different from historical patterns where negotiations occurred only one quarter in advance. As per the management, clients now want supply assurance for three to five years. This shift supports Micron with large visibility into forward revenue and pricing. This is reducing the volatility linked with memory cycles. The entire 2026 HBM supply is already sold out, thus, this backlog in my view indicates that high-margin production lines will run at maximum utilization. This will protect the gross margin profile.

Finally, I think the integration of storage into the AI architecture plays a big role, moving forward. As processors face higher loads, AI systems need tiered memory and storage. Data moves from hot tiers (HBM) to warm tiers (LPDRAM) and cold tiers (storage drives). Micron has a solid lead in the data center SSD market with products ranging from performance TLC drives to capacity QLC drives. The 232-layer tech and in-house controller capabilities allow Micron to capture value across this entire hierarchy.

Taken together, the acceleration of HBM4 production, the sold-out status of Micron’s 2026 HBM inventory, and the superior power efficiency of the company’s memory products give Micron significant pricing power. This pricing authority can continue to amplify gross margins and drive forward earnings growth rates that vastly exceed sector medians.

All Eyes on Micron’s Upcoming Q2 Earnings

The company has announced that it will release its fiscal Q2 2026 results on March 18, 2026 after market close. Micron is currently estimated to earn an EPS of $8.58 on revenue of $19.07 billion implying a 450% YoY earnings growth as the memory cycle continues to pick up with better pricing trends.

Micron's earnings growth story is also likely to be closely correlated with strong data center demand, especially for HBM products that can run alongside AI accelerators and GPUs. The company's management commentary on HBM3E shipment volumes and the ramp-up of next-generation HBM4 products will be closely watched by the market.

Micron Revenue Capped Until 2027 as 50% Demand Goes Unfilled

The main risk impacting Micron’s financials on a forward basis is a limitation on supply output with the high capital intensity required to expand production. Despite the demand for a supercycle described by management, Micron cannot fulfill the entirety of client orders.

As per Micron’s management, their key customers are only receiving 50% to 2/3rds of their demanded volume which is creating an opportunity for their competitors to gain share if they can identify the available bits. This shortage of supply, relative to the demanded volume, is being caused by the physical characteristics of manufacturing HBM. HBM is silicon-intensive, and the trade ratio for HBM3 used to be 3:1, which means one wafer of HBM requires the capacity for three wafers of standard DDR memory.

As the industry moves to HBM4, HBM4E, and HBM5, this trade ratio gets even bigger. So, the aggressive ramp of HBM production consumes a disproportionate amount of total wafer capacity. This is reducing the overall supply of bits available for other markets like PC and smartphone memory.

Micron inventories are lean mainly in DRAM. To address this issue, Micron is trying to increase bit supply through node transitions. It is specifically doing the ramp of the 1-gamma node. However, as per the management, traditional node transitions are now less efficient than in previous eras. The bit growth hit by shrinking the die size is no longer adequate to match market demand. This reality pushes Micron to depend on greenfield fabrication facilities to hit necessary supply growth. The timeline for these greenfield projects lead to a lag in revenue realization.

The new fabrication facility in Idaho (Idaho 1) may not produce meaningful volume until H2/mid-27. The newly acquired Tongluo site in Taiwan may close in Q2-26, but it will not contribute to wafer output until H2-27. The new NAND fabrication facility in Singapore may not have first wafer outs until H2-28. The mega complex in New York is scheduled for production in 2030. Thus, in essence, it implies that for the year 2026 and the beginning of 2027, Micron has few mechanisms in place to improve bit output beyond the existing capacity limits.

Finally, to fund these expansions, Micron must incur heavy CapEx. The guidance for FY26 CapEx was set at $20 billion. The acquisition of the Tongluo site adds $1.8 billion to this outlay, plus associated tool installation costs. This massive outflow of cash hits hard on FCF. TTM levered FCF Margin of 1.05% is 90.24% below the sector median. There is high percentage growth in FCF, but the absolute margin remains thin relative to the sector as massive capital is needed to build and equip semiconductor cleanrooms.

Overall, the dependency on complex node transitions and new facility construction has execution risk. Any delay in the 1-gamma ramp and the equipping of the Tongluo facility may expand the supply shortage issue. As a result, I believe the delay may hurt MU stock price based on Street’s sentiment.

Takeaway

Micron finds itself in the sweet spot in the AI-driven memory boom, with HBM4 shipments already sold out in 2026, supply constraints, and increasing hyperscale demand. This gives Micron pricing power in the constrained supply environment, and Micron will benefit from high earnings growth in the next few years.

data.title: CTO Realty Growth: Shopping For Undervalued Shares Of This Growing REIT In Southeast
data.description:
Despite the elevated volatility in markets earlier this week amidst a major geopolitical conflict going on, one retail REIT stock actually is still up over +9% in the last month, Florida-based CTO Realty Growth (CTO).

This REIT owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States and also owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust.

Since I called it a buy in my Aug. 2025 article, it is up over +19%, reflecting the market confidence in this stock, and what I liked about it then was proven AFFO growth, portfolio growth, and high dividend yield.

It also caught my attention this week after sending out a press release that it is acquiring Palms Crossing in McAllen, Texas, which is already 98% leased.

In my follow-up article today, I'm exploring this stock again across 8 rating categories and in the wake of its recent earnings results on Feb. 19th.

Thesis Summary

After my updated research this time, I am reaffirming my prior buy rating on this stock, and agreeing with the latest buy consensus from SA analysts.

The worksheet below shows how my 8 factors contributed to the holistic score:

Today's thesis argues that despite some potential macro headwinds and recessionary clouds on the horizon, as well as some weaker margins than some peers, there are several key upside drivers for this stock and they include active/robust portfolio growth along with a focus on population growth regions like the southeast US, as well as positive operating cashflow supported by the huge real estate portfolio, technical patterns favoring bullishness, and a positive price upside forecast.

Macro & Sector Outlook

When it comes to this REIT, when thinking about the big picture and what sticks out, is that it is heavily focused on the southeast US, as we see in its portfolio, along with growth in New Mexico too.

So, what comes to mind from a macro level is population growth trends driving the demand for retail shopping experiences. For instance, last autumn, a forecast published by UT San Antonio said: "driven by migration trends and suburban sprawl, Texas population expected to hit 42.6M by 2060."

In addition, regional US media reported that estimates for 2025 are that "South Carolina added around 80,000 people, which was good for a growth rate of 1.5% — the fastest in the nation." Further, Florida Realtors reported that "Florida’s population surge is expected to continue through the end of the decade."

Besides the population tailwind in the south/southeast, we should consider 2026 consumer confidence and its impact on retail, since retail properties seem to be the core focus of this REIT.

For instance, to visualize some of their properties owned, this is the Pompano City Center which has tenants like TJ Maxx, Nordstrom, and others.

Aside from grocery and pharmacy, a lot of retail falls into the consumer discretionary niche, which can be recession-prone if consumer confidence and spending ability falls. Recent geopolitical developments in the Mideast can have a negative impact, with CNBC writing this week that it "risks a major oil supply disruption in the Middle East that, in a worst-case scenario, could trigger a global economic recession."

Prior to this event, I have cited several rather positive forecasts about the 2026 economy in recent articles, and notable is that Goldman Sachs(GS) also had a positive view in January, with their chief US economist saying "our strongest conviction views for 2026 are our above-consensus GDP growth forecast and our below-consensus inflation forecast."

Therefore, considering both that and the recent elevated risk if a regional war prolongs or expands geographically and driving energy costs through the roof, but also considering southeast US population growth trends as a key tailwind, I am modestly neutral/hold in this category of macroeconomic impact.

Competitive Positioning

In terms of competitive positioning, I compared CTO vs 3 similar REIT peers due to their focus on retail properties in the US are publicly-traded: Whitestone REIT (WSR), Frontview REIT (FVR), and Realty Income (O).

Based on peer data, with an ROE of just 1.7%, CTO falls behind in this peer group, and although its 5-year revenue CAGR of nearly +22% is respectable it is 3rd in this peer group.

In terms of market momentum, compared to the performance of a major benchmark like the S&P500 over the last decade, none of these stocks beat the benchmark, and with an impressive price return of +86% CTO actually beat a much bigger peer, Realty Income.

Besides these metrics, where CTO really could continue being competitive is their focus on population growth areas like the south/southeast US, and their portfolio diversification across many states in that region.

So, an optimistic neutral/hold score is fair in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Moving on, let's consider some company growth drivers and their impact.

We already mentioned the acquisition of the retail property in McAllen TX, which I think could drive upside through future rental income.

In addition, Q4 results (pg 1) reported that in Q4 the firm "acquired Pompano Citi Centre, a 509,000 square foot open-air retail center located in a submarket of Fort Lauderdale, Florida." I think this further grows the portfolio in the Florida region, another growth area.

Besides property acquisition, we should consider new leases signed as those drive future top-line revenue. Q4 results (pg 2) said "during the year ended December 31, 2025, the Company executed 87 new leases, renewals and extensions totaling 671,000 square feet."

Another key growth lever is a strategic investment in a new Whole Foods being developed in Atlanta, next to one of CTO's existing properties, as mentioned in the Q4 investor deck (pg 12), which I think can further drive this REIT's presence in more southeast states such as Georgia.

I would also point out that Seeking Alpha data has an expected fwd revenue growth for CTO of +11.2%, easily beating the sector average, and complementing the above growth trajectory of this REIT.

Given the overwhelming growth case above, I am highly bullish in this section.

Profitability Metrics

Next, I'll consider impacts to profitability, within the context of a REIT which is a unique type of business.

The EBITDA margin is a good metric in this case, as it strips out depreciation expense and accounting noise, and CTO had an impressive margin at +62.2% but was still beaten by two peers, Realty Income and Frontview:

Another key REIT metric, the FFO (funds from operations) saw muted results for CTO, with a 5 year FFO growth CAGR of -0.80%, far worse than the sector average, while the 5-year AFFO growth was slightly better at around +1.19%, but still muted vs the sector avg. By comparison, WSR had a 5 year FFO growth CAGR of +2.4%.

A ray of light comes from the analyst consensus, though, who anticipates a YoY FFO growth of around +7% this year and next too.

What's worth mentioning is that CTO's core strategy does not seem to be that of a triple net lease REIT, so I expect it to have higher property expenses and hence impacting earnings/profitability more, particularly if there is cost inflation in property expenses.

So, following NOI (net operating income) trends could point to how efficient this REIT is at managing its real estate. There are some positive signs there, as Q4 results (pg 2) said: "for the three months ended December 31, 2025, shopping center same property NOI increased by 4.3% versus the comparable 2024 period."

Thus, given the improving NOI, positive analyst consensus, but also some weaker margins and FFO growth metrics vs peers, I gave it a reasonable neutral/hold view in this section.

Balance Sheet Risk

Equity REITs like this have real estate as their major asset, an illiquid one, and we see from balance sheet data that CTO ended FY25 with nearly $1B in real estate.

Because the REIT depends on this portfolio to generate cashflow, which should more than cover debt payments and dividends, notable from Seeking Alpha data is that core operating cashflow grew YoY by +7.9%, which I think should add some confidence to investors that this REIT is a cashflow generating engine from its properties.

On the liability side, here is how their debt/equity compares vs peers:

CTO with a D/E of 1.14 is on the higher end of this peer group in terms of leverage, and you can see in the trend line that the D/E has grown over the last year, so I think this could move the risk needle up somewhat.

Another added element of risk is that there does not appear to be any recent ratings on CTO from the major agencies (Fitch, S&P, Moody's) so I won't be including that perspective here.

What I would say, though, is that investors should consider tenant over-concentration risk. In the case of CTO, though, according to the Q4 investor deck (pg 14), we see that none of its top 10 tenants individually contribute to more than 4% of ABR (annualized base rent) so this certainly lowers the risk profile, and of them is a consumer staples category, grocery chain Publix.

Given the low tenant concentration risk and geographic portfolio diversity across multiple states, as well as the positive operating cashflow, but also considering the elevated D/E and lack of major credit ratings, I would go with a very cautious bullish score in this section.

Dividend Outlook

I've consistently written in my REIT articles, and my REIT book, that dividend income is a major point of attraction towards REIT stocks, and so notable about CTO is its above-average fwd yield of +7.7%, which should get the attention of high-yield investors.

It also impressed me with a 10-year dividend CAGR of +49.8%, which I think shows its resilience over multiple market cycles and shocks, and I'd say for this sector the key one was the global pandemic and later the elevated inflation and tariffs scare.

As far as dividend safety, consider the quarterly dividend is $0.38, and income statement data shows the AFFO per share has ranges between $0.47 - $0.50 in the last several quarters, so that implies AFFO can cover the dividend, however the AFFO payout ratio may be on the higher side, above 75%, so that also leaves less cashflow buffer and can move the risk needle higher.

Given the high yield, proven growth through various cycles and shocks, and adequate AFFO coverage, but also considering the elevated payout ratio, I think a low bullish score is fair in this section.

Valuation Metrics

In this section, the focus is on my worksheet below which uses Seeking Alpha data and compares CTO's valuation vs peers, but also uses the fwd P/FFO multiple to determine a price forecast:

My price forecast implies further upside of +20% on this stock, and compared to the latest avg Wall St forecast of nearly +13%, both support a highly bullish view.

The stock also appears undervalued vs the peer average here. That could be reasonably justified, considering the lower EBITDA margin and FFO growth I mentioned earlier, and the portfolio consisting largely of retail property and some mortgage loans, so I think the market may not be wanting to pay such a premium on this portfolio right now.

The bull case, though, comes from the future potential of CTO. The analyst consensus already has a positive FFO outlook for the end of this year and next, for instance, and NOI has improved.

Technical Charting

Coming around to the close now, I'll wrap up by considering patterns in the following technical chart which compares the share price over the last year with the 50-day and 200-day SMA:

We can see a buy signal (golden cross formation) this winter when the 50-day crossed above the 200-day. The two moving averages remain parallel, but the short term one continues upward, and the share price also is still trading above it, indicating the 50-day SMA is still acting as support.

While some may argue that this can be a neutral argument (parallel moving averages) I am going with a bullish view here and the deciding factor is the 50-day SMA providing support to what appears to be continued bullish momentum.

This technical pattern only helps further move the overall argument towards a buy rating, which is what the overall score ended up being.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

Since my view is bullish, and part of it is reliant on a growth thesis dependent on acquiring more retail properties, the limiting factor is the ability to develop new properties.

The current environment is one of high cost of capital (still elevated interest rates on CRE loans), construction cost inflation, limited and costly land to develop on, and competition from other larger REITs with capital to expand.

So, the challenge to my bullish call could come from those that argue this REIT has a limited growth runway beyond just acquiring some existing /already-built properties. The limiting factor is the cost and ability to develop new lands.

Conclusion

I've showed why I'm reaffirming my prior buy rating from 2025. Key strengths that remain are robust portfolio growth (via acquisition) in a population-growth region, NOI improvement, and dividend coverage, thereby overcoming some concerns including recession risk.

I also am including CTO in my dividend ideas for March, due to its high yield, coverage, and proven growth.

data.title: Broadcom: Blowout Earnings Add Even More Conviction
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) delivered blowout fiscal Q1 earnings yesterday after the market close, fortifying the long-term bullish case once again. The company's AI-related revenue continues soaring, while profitability is also expanding aggressively. The management's tone regarding AI-related business during the earnings call was very confident, which means that the company is likely to maintain its aggressive revenue and EPS for longer.

The stock spiked post-earnings by more than 6%, and I consider the market's reaction absolutely reasonable. Broadcom's share price didn't change much since my previous writeup, which means that valuation remains extremely attractive. I believe AVGO remains a Strong Buy.

FQ1 2026 earnings review

Broadcom's FQ1 2026 earnings were marked with another double beat. Both revenue and EPS were higher than consensus estimates, which was achieved for the fifth quarter in a row. Consolidated revenue growth accelerated to 29.5% on a year-over-year basis, which was the fourth quarter in a row with accelerating top line growth. Broadcom's non-GAAP EPS increased almost at the same rate as revenue did, increasing from $1.60 to $2.05. It was the record-breaking quarter for the company both in terms of revenue and EPS.

The company generated adjusted EBITDA of $13.1 billion, which yields a staggering 67.9% margin. It implies that operating leverage is still strong, and the management expects even a slight expansion to 68% in FQ2 according to the guidance. By the way, revenue guidance of $22 billion for the next quarter was almost 10% higher than Wall Street analysts expected. With that being said, Broadcom is expected to continue breaking its own revenue records further.

It is also vital to say that the forecasted FQ2 2026 revenue of $22 billion means that year-over-year growth will accelerate significantly in the next quarter. The company generated $15 billion in sales in FQ2 2025, which means we will likely see a staggering ~47% year-over-year revenue growth during the next earnings season.

Such an impressive revenue growth acceleration is explained by Broadcom's stellar ability to capitalize on AI infrastructure tailwinds. During the earnings call, the management cited a massive 106% year-over-year growth in AI-related sales. This impressive momentum in AI revenue growth will also accelerate in FQ2 as the management expects a 140% year-over year increase to $10.7 billion. Such a rapid growth in AI related revenue is very impressive considering the fact that Broadcom's consolidated quarterly sales surpassed $10 billion just two years ago, in FQ1 2024. And now, the AI business of the company alone generates approximately the same revenue as this entire giant did just eight quarters ago.

Such an aggressive forecast for AI-related revenue doesn't look unrealistic because of the fact that the fierce data center race between tech giants continues. All of the largest public players in generative AI (Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, and Google) confirmed during the Q4 2025 earnings season that they are ready to significantly boost investments in AI infrastructure during calendar 2026. Moreover, some of these companies also expressed opinions that investments in data center will continue growing in 2027 as well. Broadcom already boasts partnerships and contracts with all the major companies excelling in generative AI, meaning that it will certainly capitalize on this massive structural tailwind. During the earnings call, the management cited tougher and tougher competitive environment, but AVGO's $10+ billion annual R&D budget together with stellar record of success gives me confidence in its ability to protect its moat.

Finally, the bullish outlook is also fortified by the fact that Broadcom plans to continue its shareholder-friendly capital allocation with dividend increases and buybacks. During the call, the management announced that the Board of Directors has authorized additional share repurchase program worth $10 billion. This is another positive factor for investors as it reinforces the management's confidence in Broadcom's growth strategy which will anchor stability in cash generating capacity.

Valuation analysis

Broadcom's share price did not change much since my previous bullish writeup while there were multiple upward EPS revisions. It means that AVGO's forward P/E ratio setup has become even more attractive. The table below compares Broadcom's current forward P/E ratio setup to the one that was actual two months ago.

As we can see, AVGO's forward P/E ratios for FY2026-2028 compressed by double-digits, which is a substantial change. It means that upward earnings revisions for the next three fiscal years expanded by double-digits while share price remained flat. It is also vital to say that Broadcom's EPS is expected to significantly outpace the sector as its current PEG ratio of 0.95 is almost 30% lower compared to the sector.

Broadcom's PEG ratio estimate is based on consensus EPS forecasts, which doesn't guarantee success. However, Broadcom's flawless earnings beat record significantly increases the probability of actual results beating Wall Street forecasts.

Finally, the consensus target price of $455 is massively higher than AVGO's current share price even after the post-earnings rally. The most fascinating part is that even the lowest Wall Street target price estimate of $335 is approximately in line with the pre-market share price while I am writing this analysis.

Mitigating factors

The global geopolitical landscape is rapidly evolving in 2026 as the U.S. continues conducting its military operations. And if the military operation in Venezuela was quick and smooth, it is difficult to say the same about Iran because it is a large country with 90 million population and quite strong army. Moreover, the number of countries indirectly involved in this military operation is increasing as Iran continues expanding geography of its missile and drone launches. For example, just a few hours ago Iranian drones struck one of Azerbaijan's airports.

If this military operation continues longer-than-expected, it might adversely affect AVGO in two ways. First and foremost, investors are likely to become more fearful, which means a potential selloff in growth stocks. Second, it will disrupt global supply chains, which might affect operations of Broadcom or its key global partners. We see that oil prices have already spiked due to the military escalation, which is another adverse factor for the entire stock market because higher energy prices will push inflation up again.

Conclusion

AVGO's impressive FQ1 2026 earnings increase my conviction in bullish outlook for this stock. Moreover, the stock remains very attractively valued, which makes me reiterate the Strong Buy rating.

data.title: FSCO: High-Quality BDCs Have Spoiled Me, Hence I'm Still Avoiding This Pick
data.description:
In June 2025, I published my first article on FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO), which was a bullish one. The reason why I back then assigned a Buy rating was related mainly to conservative leverage and attractive floating vs. fixed-rate loan book positioning amid a declining interest rate environment.

However, a couple of months later, seeing that FSCO had significantly outperformed the Business Development Company ("BDC") index (BIZD) - by about 14% on a total return basis - and also recognizing that the Fund had an exposure to bankrupt First Brands business, I thought to myself that it was a timely moment to exit. The increasingly stretched dividend coverage was also what motivated me to assume a bearish stance, as outlined in the article FSCO: Big Downside Risk And Almost Certain Yield Cut (Downgrade). Finally, the presence of some similar yield but higher quality BDC alternatives made the choice even easier.

Since that moment, it has been "nothing but net" for my thesis:

Now, such kinds of drawdowns open the door for some logical questions around too aggressive discounting and thus potentially compelling entry points. These could be my initial thoughts from the bull's perspective:

  • FSCO yields 15.5%, which is the highest yield it ever has provided.
  • The Fund trades at ~27% discount to NAV.
  • The ~20% delta in total return performance relative to BDCs is interesting, potentially unjustified given the various overlaps between them.

However, in all fairness, I do not think that it is the right time to enter FSCO.

Let me flesh out the reasoning below.

Thesis review

First of all, I would like to establish an important context, which is that I am a structural private credit and/or BDC bull. Many of my closest followers will definitely agree with that. Also, my recent strategic piece on the BDC landscape, I think, clearly confirms that as well: Stop Panicking And Start Buying BDCs.

However, being a structural bull on a particular sector does not mean that everything that comes with a label that is associated with that particular sector is a buy. Actually, it is the opposite. The more advanced one becomes, the selectivity and taste tend to get more granular, nuanced, and, perhaps, spoiled (in a good sense).

So, with this being said, here is why FSCO is too risky for me and, also, from the P/NAV perspective, arguably, still too expensive.

There are 5 important aspects.

First, FSCO is not as cheap as it might seem. Yes, on an absolute level, ~27% discount is quite big, but in relative terms, the distance between FSCO valuation and BDC median valuation is not that wide. Namely, the median BDC trades at a 24% discount to NAV.

So, from this, I think we can put the argument of FSCO being relatively attractively valued aside and focus on the underlying fundamentals, which could theoretically support a greater valuation than for the median BDC.

Second, from the portfolio construction perspective, I would not give any points to FSCO, nor would I penalize it.

About 83% of the total portfolio is placed in senior secured first lien loans, which is the safest area to be from the lender's perspective. However, 83% in first lien means that 17% is somewhere else, where the risks are inherently higher. For example, we have second lien loans, unsecured debt, equity holdings, etc. In times like these, I would try to skew my allocations towards the safest possible area - i.e., senior secured first lien credit with at least 90% of the total exposure.

Third, and this is what I have indirectly implied from FSCO's portfolio in-place spread level, the underlying loans are much riskier than for most BDCs.

For example, the 2025 loan investments that FSCO made were underwritten at an average spread of SOFR + 661 bps. This kind of spread is what we don't see among high-quality BDC names and even not in the LMM private credit area, where the compensation for risk tends to be higher. The average spread that we can basically see across the board in the BDC space is between 475 bps and 575 bps over SOFR. Anything that is above is usually associated with a second lien, PIK or ARR loan.

CION Investment Corporation (CION) is a good example, which has a huge concentration of PIK and thus closer spread levels to FSCO than say for top-tier names such as Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) and Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC). But this is also why CION trades at a P/NAV of 0.56x. So, as we know, the higher the risk, the higher the spreads.

Fourth, the earnings quality confirms the previous point. FSCO has 21.7% of total income coming in the form of PIK. It is a very, very high number. Much higher than the BDC sector average of ~7.5%.

There are three BDCs that have their PIK figures in 20%+ territory: TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG), Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) and CION. And this is how their share prices have evolved since 2024:

It looks like FSCO has some catching up to do.

Fifth, FSCO has a deeply undercovered dividend. In Q4 2025, FSCO generated NII per share of $0.12, while the total distributions that were paid during that quarter amounted to slightly above $0.20 per share.

The bottom line

Even though I think that the fears about private credit exploding are way overblown and the corresponding BDC (private credit) discounts have become extremely compelling, it does not mean that everything in this complex is a buy.

We have to respect the market and understand that, while it is indeed emotional, there is usually some valid ground behind its actions or reactions to incoming data. In other words, it is time to buy BDCs, but with caution and a defensive bias.

In this respect, FSCO is anything but defensive. The combination of massive PIK, meaningful concentration outside of senior secured first lien credit, and aggressive spreads indicates that the risk level here is higher than what we can get in the BDC space, where the average discounts are rather similar to that of FSCO.

I am still avoiding FSCO after the Q4 2025 report.

data.title: Allspring California Tax-Free Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund Strategy

  • Uses both bottom-up credit research and top-down macroeconomic analysis
  • Seeks to generate excess performance by actively managing the four key elements of total return: duration, yield-curve positioning, sector and credit-quality allocation, and security selection
  • Uses a relative-value approach based on extensive credit analysis that seeks opportunities from changing market trends and pricing inefficiencies to generate excess returns

Average Annual Total Returns (%) As Of 12/31/2025*

Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended December 31, 2025.
  • Duration and yield-curve positioning were the largest contributors to performance while sector and credit-quality allocation detracted.

Market review

Municipal bonds had a strong fourth quarter, posting a positive return of 1.56%. This outpaced most taxable fixed income asset classes. Only U.S. mortgage-backed securities (MBS) delivered a stronger fourth quarter return at 1.71%. Outside of MBS and tax-exempt municipals, the next best-performing fixed income asset classes were taxable municipals (1.37%) and U.S. commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS, 1.34%). While still positive, the 10-year Treasury and U.S. corporates had the lowest fixed income returns for the quarter at 0.83% and 0.84%, respectively.

Within municipal credit tiers, BBB-rated bonds performed the best for the quarter, returning 1.77%. Interestingly, high yield municipal debt was the worst-performing municipal credit sector, though it still posted a positive return of 1.15%. For the year, returns were similar across all investment-grade municipal debt, with AAA- through BBB-rated bonds returning between 4.35% and 4.13%. The outlier for the year was high yield municipal debt, which returned just 2.46%. The high yield municipal sector was weighed down by a high-profile credit that experienced a technical default and was downgraded multiple notches. Despite this underperformance, municipal high yield spreads remained rich relative to historical averages.

The municipal AAA yield curve flattened further in the fourth quarter, continuing the trend that began in September. While most of the curve flattening in the quarter happened in the first three weeks of October, the combined September/October flattening move was enough to completely unwind the steepening that took place in the first eight months of the year. The AAA municipal 2-year/10-year yield curve did move marginally steeper in November and December to finish the year very close to where it started.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered its benchmark interest rate at both its October and December meetings. This combined 50-basis-point (bp; 100 bps equal 1.00%) decrease left the overnight federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%. With most estimates of the neutral rate somewhere between 2.75% and 3.25%, the market is pricing in just over two more cuts in 2026, which would bring the benchmark rate close to neutral. This bias for lower rates, combined with a more dovish FOMC chair coming in May, leads us to expect the yield curve to steepen again in 2026.

Fund attribution and strategy

Duration—contributor

  • The fund was long duration relative to the benchmark during the period, which contributed to performance as yields moved lower for maturities beyond five years.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. October municipal performance was particularly strong, especially given the typical weakness we usually see during the large primary issuance calendar in October. While still positive, November and December returns were more subdued, as some of the October performance pulled forward gains from year-end.
  • As we head into 2026, we are expecting another year of strong inflows, offset by another year of record primary issuance. We believe that attractive valuations relative to other fixed income asset classes, along with still-compelling tax-exempt yields, should drive another year of solid performance. Like 2025, we believe income will be the main driver of returns, as rate volatility could increase with a new FOMC chair.

Yield curve—contributor

  • Returns were strongest in the 15- to 20-year maturity segment. The long end of the curve outperformed the index average and the front end of the curve underperformed as short rates moved higher. The fund benefited from an overweight to longer maturities and an underweight to shorter maturities.
  • The municipal benchmark curve saw an “operation twist” rewind in October. The front end of the yield curve moved higher in yield, while the long end continued the September rally. The 1-year/30-year AAA municipal curve ended the third quarter at 192 basis points and flattened to a low of 153 bps in November. We did see the curve steepen again in December, to close the year at 165 bps.
  • We believe the increase in front-end municipal yields was more technical in nature (accounts were overweight and the sector had become rich), and the new FOMC chair will be supportive for front-end rates (low likelihood of an increase in overnight rates).

For these reasons, we think the bias will be for the yield curve to steepen again in 2026, continuing the trend that started in December.

Quality—modest detractor

  • Returns were positive across the credit spectrum but were somewhat mixed during the period. BBB rated bonds were the top performing and A rated were the bottom performing during the period. The fund remains overweight A/BBB and underweight AAA/AA.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the recent downgrade of Brightline demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—detractor

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Top-performing sectors in the California Index were local GO (underweight), transportation (underweight), and hospital (overweight). Bottom-performing sectors were industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (overweight), special tax (overweight), and education (overweight).
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts, along with essential services provided by municipalities, are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Security selection—modest contributor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was positive for the quarter. Despite being overweight bottom-performing sectors in the California Index, the fund’s exposure to these sectors outperformed similar bonds held in the index.
  • The volatility observed throughout the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

data.title: Sea: Steep Investments Necessary To Defend Leadership - Maintain Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Sea Limited (SE) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating upon a further moderation to the $108s, with the much-needed meltdown already triggering a notably improved margin of safety from the cheaper valuations and the expanded capital appreciation prospects.

Combined with the robust performance metrics observed in each of the three segments and the synergistic nature of the eCommerce/fintech segments, I was of the opinion that the well-diversified company's investment thesis had improved drastically, despite the falling knife risks.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the SE stock after the materialization of a double bottom, thanks to the improved margin of safety from the recent selloff and the consequently cheaper valuations.

This is significantly aided by the diversified company's high growth top-line prospects and the still resilient profit margins despite the intensified growth initiatives, as the management attempts to drive market share gains, supported by the richer free cash flow generation and the healthier balance sheet.

SE Defends eCommerce Leadership In SEA

For now, SE's correction has occurred, albeit steeper than expected, as the stock lost nearly all of their Q4'24-Q3'25 gains to retest the prior support levels of $80s by the time of writing, with a similar pullback also observed in many of its diversified commerce peers in varying degrees.

This is worsened by the market overreacting to SE's supposedly drastic increase in expenses and provision for credit losses for the Monee segment, with it contributing to the steep one-day correction by -16.5% post FQ4'25 earnings call.

For now, I am of the opinion that the selloff has been overly done indeed, as the market notably discounts SE's undeniably robust performance metrics across the eCommerce and gaming segments.

1. Monee

For example, the source of the pessimism is attributed to Monee's drastic increase in expenses, which contributed to the digital financial services' deteriorating adjusted EBITDA margins of 23.9% in FQ4'25 (-2.1 points QoQ/-4.8 YoY/-6.6 from the FY2023 highs of 30.5%).

The deterioration in its profit margins may be jarring in comparison to the segment's accelerating top-line growth to $1.1B in FQ4'25 (+54.3% YoY) and the growing consumer/SME loans to $9.2B (+80.4%), as the SE management seemingly focuses on market share gains at a time of intensifying fintech competition (including from Grab Holdings (GRAB)) while putting their bottom-line performance on the back seat.

Given that Monee used to be a bottom-line driver in FY2023 before the eCommerce segment turned profitable, I can understand why the market has overreacted indeed, especially since their provision for credit losses has grown alongside the loan book to $393.1M (+66.7% YoY), with it presenting the mixed optics to its digital financial services' performance metrics.

Otherwise, readers only need to focus on their moderating non-performing loans ratio of 1.1% in FQ4'25 (inline QoQ/-0.1 points YoY) to understand that Monee's loan book remains extremely healthy thus far.

This is especially since SE has reported great success in growing the use-cases of Monee beyond the Shopee ecosystem, as observed in the off-Shopee SPayLater loans growing by +300% YoY, while attracting 5.8M of unique first-time borrowers in FQ4'25 and growing their active credit users to 37M (+40% YoY).

These developments lend credence to the segment's high-growth prospects, albeit with further near-term bottom-line noise likely as the management hints at further "progress growing our products and services beyond credit from digital banking to insurance and more."

2. Garena/Shopee

I am of the opinion that Monee's deteriorating adj. EBITDA margins have been well balanced by SE's gaming segment, Garena, expanding adj. EBITDA margins (to bookings) of 54.1% in FQ4'25 (+0.8 points YoY) and 56.1% in FY2025 (+0.3 points YoY/+5.2 from FY2023 levels of 50.9%).

Much of the expanding profitability is thanks to the improved operating leverage as they grow their quarterly paying users to 58M (+15% YoY) and their paying user ratio to 9.2% (+1 point YoY), significantly aided by the expanding bookings per user at $1.06 (+20.4% YoY).

At the same time, Shopee has further entrenched itself as the eCommerce market leader, as observed in the expanding regional share to 52% in 2024 (+4 points YoY) despite the intensifying competition from ByteDance's (BDNCE) TikTok, Alibaba's (BABA) Lazada, and Amazon (AMZN), among others.

The same has been observed in Shopee's growing orders by +30.5% YoY and the higher Gross Merchandise Volumes by +28.6% YoY, all of which contribute to the segment's expanding marketplace revenues of $4.3B (+34.3% YoY).

Much of SE's success is attributed to their diversified logistics approach for different markets, such as instant/same-day delivery services for urban areas and economical shipping for suburban areas, allowing them to drive improved penetration across buyers and sellers - all of which feeding its vertically integrated eCommerce, logistics, and fintech growth flywheel.

This is on top of the management taking a leaf out of AMZN's Amazon Prime playbook, with the introduction of Shopee VIP membership program being well received, as observed in the accelerating member growth to over 7M by the end of the 2025 year, despite the recent launch in Q1'25.

Given the relatively low penetration rate of ~1.7% at 400M of its active buyers as of 2025, I am of the opinion that the eCommerce segment has further room for growth.

This is especially since VIP members typically spend more at higher frequency, allowing Shopee to incrementally gain market share in an increasingly competitive eCommerce market:

And what we are doing in 2026 is essentially to reach out those users to convert them to our platforms and hence, enhance the overall -- the MTUs -- the MAUs for our platforms. So that's kind of a broader theme of what we are doing in South Asia. (Seeking Alpha)

3. Mixed FY2026 Guidance

Part of the selloff may also be attributed to SE's mixed FY2026 guidance with Shopee GMV growth of +25% YoY albeit with a "full year adjusted EBITDA no lower than that of 2025 in absolute dollar terms," with it implying a deteriorating adj EBITDA margins.

When compared against the overall company's adj EBITDA margins of 11.4% in FQ4'25 (-0.4 points YoY) and 14.8% in FY2025 (+2.9 points YoY/+5.7 from FY2023 levels of 9.1%, the first year that they are profitable on adj EBITDA basis), it is unsurprising that the market has turned pessimistic.

These developments imply SE's intensified efforts to stave off the growing eCommerce/fintech competition from other players, as Momentum Works also report TikTok Shop's doubled GMV in Southeast Asia [SEA] in 2025.

While their "calculations also suggest Shopee remained ahead in order and parcel volumes – but the gap was clearly narrowing across the region" and TikTok shop expected to "catches up with Shopee in parcel volume" in 2026, I can understand the market's decision to de-risk and sell first.

This is especially since it remains to be seen how long SE's intensified investments and the consequently, impacted adj EBITDA margins may last.

The same caution has also embedded in the consensus latest forward estimates, with SE expected to report a mixed top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +20.9%/+27.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +17.3%/+33.3% while notably decelerating from the 2Y historical growth profile of +32.5%/+101.2% (the period of time that they are adj EPS profitable), respectively.

These reasons may also be why SE has been notably downgraded to FY2025 EV/Sales valuations of 1.95x and FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $44.87B and the FY2025 revenues of $22.93B along the current stock prices of $87.82 and the FY2025 adj EPS of $3.17.

This is down from the recent highs of 4.56x/41.22x and the 5Y mean of 5.49x/38.21x, albeit still somewhat inflated against the sector median of 1.28x/15.95x, respectively.

Given their high growth, top-line cadence, I am of the opinion that SE is extremely compelling here, as similarly observed in my estimated FY2028 EV/Sales valuations of 1.11x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $44.87B and the consensus FY2028 revenue estimates of $40.1B.

Based on the FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x and the consensus ajd EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +27.5%, the stock is still attractive at my estimated 3Y PEG ratio of 1.00x, compared to the sector median of 1.65x.

When comparing SE's FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x and my estimated 3Y PEG ratio of 1.00x to its well-diversified commerce peers, including:

  • AMZN at 27x/1.52x (diversified, including cloud and advertising),
  • MercadoLibre (MELI) at 31.03x/0.80x (diversified, including fintech), and
  • BABA at 22.62x/4.52x (diversified, including cloud and entertainment), respectively,

it is apparent that SE's intensified growth initiatives and the consequently downgraded consensus adj EPS estimates have triggered near-term noise to its investment thesis, albeit with the steep correction bringing the stock nearer to its peer group P/E mean of ~27x.

Thanks to the richer Free Cash Flow generation of $4.51B (+52.3% YoY), readers may want to note that SE already reports an extremely healthy balance sheet at a net cash position of $8.72B (+54.8% YoY) and a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of -2.54x in FY2025 (given the higher cash than debt).

When compared to AMZN at -0.39x in FY2025, MELI at 1.04x in FY2025, and BABA at -1.72x in FQ3'25 (given the higher cash than debt), I am of the opinion that SE remains well capitalized to strategically invest in their growth opportunities despite the near-term margin headwinds.

SE Remains A Great Buy, After A Double Bottom

For now, it remains to be seen when the bulls may defend a bottom, as SE lost -55.9% of its value since the 52 week highs while consistently running away from the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

As a result of the steep selloff, it is unsurprising that SE has already entered oversold territories, as observed in the low RSIs and trading volumes, as the stock retests the August 2022/Q2'23 resistance levels of $80s by the time of writing.

Based on the FY2025 P/E non-GAAP valuations of 27.70x (near to the peer group mean of ~27x) and the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $6.57, there remains an excellent upside potential of +107% to my long-term price target of $181.90, with it apparent that the correction has been a boon for those looking to add.

As a result of the improved margin of safety from the recent selloff, their high-growth top-line prospects, and the cheap FY2028 EV/Sales valuations, along with their ability to incrementally invest in their growth initiatives thanks to the richer free cash flow generation and the healthier balance sheet, I am maintaining my buy rating for the SE stock.

This is preferably after the bulls defend a double bottom at the $80s, barring which, a further retracement to the next support levels of the $70s is not overly bearish, given the currently pessimistic stock market sentiments.

data.title: Kearny Financial: Improving Profitability, But Still Not Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to my previous analysis of Kearny Financial (KRNY), the New Jersey-based holding company for Kearny Bank. When I first wrote about KRNY in early October of last year, I rated the stock as a hold. While the company was trading at a low valuation, I had concerns at the time about declining net interest income, overall uneven earnings trends, and relatively low net interest margin. Fast forward to today, and Kearny has posted its first two quarterly results for FY 2026, and its stock is up almost 17% since that previous article was written.

Not all of the issues that I mentioned in October have been fixed completely, but the company’s bottom line appears to be on a stronger trajectory for the second half of its fiscal year. Credit quality appears to be solid, and the bank could be a takeover candidate for a larger bank looking for access to the New Jersey market. Unfortunately, there is still just not enough quality in the operations of the company for me to raise my opinion of its stock, but I would say that it is closer to a buy than it was last fall.

Company Overview

Kearny Bank is a small-cap community bank that operates 40 branch locations in New Jersey and metropolitan New York City, with the bulk of those being in the Garden State, as shown below. KRNY has a current market cap of $516.3 million. The bank has a history that dates back over 140 years. While much of the bank’s growth in assets over the last 15 years can be attributed to acquisitions, it has been almost six years now since it was involved in M&A activity.

At the end of 2025, Kearny Financial reported ownership of $7.6 billion in assets, $5.8 billion of which were comprised of outstanding loans. (The company’s fiscal year ends on June 30, so the most recent quarterly report was for Q2 2026.) A breakdown of that loan portfolio is shown below. Over 55% of the bank’s loans are connected to properties in New Jersey. An additional 32.8% correspond to assets in New York. The size of the loan portfolio has decreased 0.8% when compared to Q2 2025.

The largest segment of the bank’s loans is commercial multifamily loans at 45.5%, a 1.1% decrease from last October’s report. Residential mortgages have stayed consistent over the last two quarters at 30.1%. Commercial real estate has the bulk of the remaining loans outstanding at 17.2%. While the relatively low amount of CRE reduces some of the risk taken on by the bank, it also lowers its average loan yield, which was 4.67% at the end of Q2 2026. The most recent report from the FDIC states that the average loan yield for a bank of Kearny’s size is 6.56%. KRNY’s underperformance here is something the company has committed to working on in the year ahead, but improvements will not come quickly.

Kearny Financial has a current quarterly dividend payout of $0.11, an amount that has not changed since Q4 2021. Its stock currently yields 5.36%, well above the median for other peers from its sector. As I pointed out in my initial analysis, KRNY gets low marks in dividend safety, as its payout ratio is over twice the norm for the last 12 months.

Analysis of Kearny Financial

Kearny Financial last reported its quarterly results on January 22. KRNY earned $0.15 per diluted share, matching its results from Q1 2026. Considering the company earned $0.42 per share in FY 2025, these results from the first two quarters put Kearny on track for overall earnings growth of over 42%, year-over-year. However, KRNY has a long way to go before it can match its results from FY 2022, when it reported a profit of $0.92 per share.

Beyond just the improvement in profits, what has caught my attention are trends that are moving in the right direction under the surface. Below are several other highlights from Q2 2026.

  • Net interest margin expanded by four basis points to 2.14%. This marked the fifth straight quarter of margin improvement dating back to Q2 2025. However, this number is still well below the average for a regional bank.
  • Net interest income, which was a point of concern from my previous article, increased 16.4% in Q2 compared to the previous year. For the first six months of FY 2026, net interest income is up 16.3% when compared to the same time period in FY 2025.
  • Non-performing assets decreased 20.6% to $51.3 million, or 0.67% of total assets, showing improvement after a large increase in Q1 2026 that was related to one loan that has since been repaid.
  • Net charge offs decreased from 0.07% to 0.05%, as shown below.

The bank’s balance of loans to deposits has been improving in the first six months of FY 2026. Total loans outstanding have decreased slightly from $5.81 billion to $5.75 billion, a decline of about 1%. Total deposits have grown only 0.6%, but much more significant improvements have been made in the category that is most valuable to the bank: non-interest-bearing deposits. This type of deposit has increased $45.1 million, or 7.8%, in the first six months of the current fiscal year. This trend, along with an overall increase in the bank’s loan yield, should help continue the much needed improvements in the bank’s net interest margin.

When I first wrote about Kearny Financial, its stock was trading at only 0.68x its tangible book value. Today that discount is 0.78x. However, the tangible book value for the company is increasing at a pedestrian rate, less than 1% in the last quarter. The performance metrics below help explain much of the reason for the discount, as the typical bank trades around 1.4x or 1.5x TBV per share.

National Average
